title: Offshore platform system monitoring

compliance.assurance_rationale: NO Petroleumsforskriften §15 — UC provides continuous Splunk telemetry evidence for this regulatory requirement, uplifted from contributing to partial after review confirmed the SPL captures relevant monitoring signals.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NO Petroleumsforskriften §15 (Health, safety and environmental (HSE) management requirements) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.26.11: Offshore platform system monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.26.11' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621)
spl:
index=ot sourcetype="engineering:plc" asset="NOR-OFF-*" earliest=-7d
| eval wo=coalesce(work_order,"NONE")
| join type=left asset [| search index=change sourcetype="snow:chg" earliest=-30d | rename cmdb_ci as asset | stats latest(number) as chg by asset]
| where wo="NONE" AND isnull(chg)
| stats latest(program_hash) as phash by asset, program_name, user
description: Monitors historian and controller tag changes that could impact process safety on offshore installations.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NO Petroleumsforskriften compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes change, ot and sourcetypes engineering:plc, snow:chg. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Maintain supporting lookups (classified hosts, sectors, transfers) aligned to Norwegian regulatory language. (2) Schedule searches with Europe/Oslo time semantics for reporting cutoffs. (3) Route ES notables to the correct national security, OT, or privacy queues. (4) Quarterly false-positive tuning with HR, operations, and legal stakeholders. Indexes required: `index=change`, `index=ot`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=engineering:plc`, `sourcetype=snow:chg`.
visualization: Bar chart (events by host), Table (top users), Single value (policy violations).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user | sort - count

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux

knownFalsePositives: DSA reporting during scheduled control reviews, planned exception cycles, or after major regulatory changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain supporting lookups (classified hosts, sectors, transfers) aligned to Norwegian regulatory language. (2) Schedule searches with Europe/Oslo time semantics for reporting cutoffs. (3) Route ES notables to the correct national security, OT, or privacy queues. (4) Quarterly false-positive tuning with HR, operations, and legal stakeholders.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=ot sourcetype="engineering:plc" asset="NOR-OFF-*" earliest=-7d
| eval wo=coalesce(work_order,"NONE")
| join type=left asset [| search index=change sourcetype="snow:chg" earliest=-30d | rename cmdb_ci as asset | stats latest(number) as chg by asset]
| where wo="NONE" AND isnull(chg)
| stats latest(program_hash) as phash by asset, program_name, user
```

Understanding this SPL

**Offshore platform system monitoring (PSA)** — Monitors historian and controller tag changes that could impact process safety on offshore installations.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: ot; **sourcetype**: engineering:plc. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=ot, sourcetype="engineering:plc", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **wo** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Correlates data from a subsearch to enrich the primary result set with additional context.
• Filters the current rows with `where wo="NONE" AND isnull(chg)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` aggregates events by **asset, program_name, user**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Offshore platform system monitoring (PSA)** — Monitors historian and controller tag changes that could impact process safety on offshore installations.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Authentication.Authentication` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.

Output fields: asset, asset], chg, phash, program_name, user, wo.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the GRC system and the audit register, and with regional reporting cutoffs in your runbook. Document any expected regulatory reporting window or approved exception before escalating.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (events by host), Table (top users), Single value (policy violations).
grandmaExplanation: We help you show national security and critical-sector monitoring rules are met with the events you keep in one place for review.
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for Authentication (CIM data model).
